analogy: Think of "The Age of Industrialisation" through the chapter's own opening images: 79 The Age of Industrialisation T h e A g e o f I n d us t r i a l isa t i o n Chapter IV The Age of Industrialisation The Age of Industrialisation In 1900, a popular music publisher E.T. Paull... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 1 Before the Industrial Revolution, 1.1 The Coming Up of the Factory, 1.2 The Pace of Industrial Change, 2 Hand Labour and Steam Power. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
The Age of Industrialisation is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that 79 The Age of Industrialisation T h e A g e o f I n d us t r i a l isa t i o n Chapter IV The Age of Industrialisation The Age of Industrialisation In 1900, a popular music publisher E.T.

The text explains that paull produced a music book that had a picture on the cover page announcing the ‘Dawn of the Century’ (Fig.

The text explains that as you can see from the illustration, at the centre of the picture is a goddess-like figure, the angel of progress, bearing the flag of the new century.

The text explains that she is gently perched on a wheel with wings, symbolising time.

The text explains that floating about, behind her, are the signs of progress: railway, camera, machines, printing press and factory.

The text explains that this glorification of machines and technology is even more marked in a picture which appeared on the pages of a trade magazine over a hundred years ago (Fig.

The text explains that the one at the top is Aladdin from the Orient who built a beautiful palace with his New words Orient – The countries to the east of the Mediterranean, usually referring to Asia.

The text explains that the term arises out of a western viewpoint that sees this region as pre- modern, traditional and mysterious Fig.

The text explains that india and the Contemporary World 80 Give two examples where modern development that is associated with progress has led to problems.

The text explains that you may like to think of areas related to environmental issues, nuclear weapons or disease.

The text explains that the one at the bottom is the modern mechanic, who with his modern tools weaves a new magic: builds bridges, ships, towers and high-rise buildings.

The text explains that aladdin is shown as representing the East and the past, the mechanic stands for the West and modernity.

The text explains that these images offer us a triumphant account of the modern world.

The text explains that within this account the modern world is associated with rapid technological change and innovations, machines and factories, railways and steamships.

The text explains that the history of industrialisation thus becomes simply a story of development, and the modern age appears as a wonderful time of technological progress.

The text explains that these images and associations have now become part of popular imagination.

The text explains that do you not think that the spread of railways and factories, and construction of high-rise buildings and bridges is a sign of society’s development?

The text explains that to answer such questions we need to turn to the history of industrialisation.

The text explains that in this chapter we will look at this history by focusing first on Britain, the first industrial nation, and then India, where the pattern of industrial change was conditioned by colonial rule.

The text explains that rulers granted different guilds the monopoly right to produce and trade in specific products.

The text explains that cottagers and poor peasants who had earlier depended on common lands for their survival, gathering their firewood, berries, vegetables, hay and straw, had to now look for alternative sources of income.

The text explains that it also allowed them a fuller use of their family labour resources.

The text explains that within this system a close relationship developed between the town and the countryside.

The text explains that this proto-industrial system was thus part of a network of commercial exchanges.

The text explains that this increase was linked to a number of changes within the process of production.

The text explains that a series of inventions in the eighteenth century increased the efficacy of each step of the production process (carding, twisting and spinning, and rolling).

The text explains that then Richard Arkwright created the cotton mill.

The text explains that within the mill all the New words Stapler – A person who ‘staples’ or sorts wool according to its fibre Fuller – A person who ‘fulls’ – that is, gathers – cloth by pleating Carding – The process in which fibres, such as cotton or wool, are prepared prior to spinning Fig.

The text explains that 83 The Age of Industrialisation processes were brought together under one roof and management.

The text explains that this allowed a more careful supervision over the production process, a watch over quality, and the regulation of labour, all of which had been difficult to do when production was in the countryside.

The text explains that seemingly ordinary and small innovations were the basis of growth in many non-mechanised sectors such as food processing, building, pottery, glass work, tanning, furniture making, and production of implements.

The text explains that source Source A A range of products could be produced only with hand labour.

The text explains that explain in your letter what you have heard and why you do not wish to invest in the new technology.

The text explains that source Source B New words Spinning Jenny – Devised by James Hargreaves in 1764, this machine speeded up the spinning process and reduced labour demand.

The text explains that explain why many workers were opposed to the use of the Spinning Jenny.

The text explains that the European companies gradually gained power – first securing a variety of concessions from local courts, then the monopoly rights to trade.

The text explains that however, once the East India Company established political power, it could assert a monopoly right to trade.

The text explains that it proceeded to develop a system of management and control that would eliminate competition, control costs, and ensure regular supplies of cotton and silk goods.

The text explains that first: the Company tried to eliminate the existing traders and brokers connected with the cloth trade, and establish a more direct control over the weaver.

The text explains that one way of doing this was through the system of advances.

The text explains that weaving, in fact, required the labour of the entire family, with children and women all engaged in different stages of the process.

The text explains that they pressurised the government to impose import duties on cotton textiles so that Manchester goods could sell in Britain without facing any competition from outside.

The text explains that krishnamurty, ‘Deindustrialisation in Gangetic Bihar during the nineteenth century’, The Indian Economic and Social History Review, 1985.

The text explains that source Source C Reporting on the Koshtis, a community of weavers, the Census Report of Central Provinces stated: ‘The Koshtis, like the weavers of the finer kinds of cloth in other parts of India, have fallen upon evil times.

The text explains that they are unable to compete with the showy goods which Manchester sends in such profusion, and they have of late years emigrated in great numbers, chiefly to Berar, where as day labourers they are able to obtain wages …’ Census Report of Central Provinces, 1872, quoted in Sumit Guha, ‘The handloom industry in Central India, 1825-1950’, The Indian Economic and Social History Review.

The text explains that this marked the decline of the old trading order and the growth of the colonial economy.

The text explains that the history of many business groups goes back to trade with China.

The text explains that 95 The Age of Industrialisation commercial groups, but they were not directly involved in external trade.

The text explains that source Source E Bhai Bhosle, a trade unionist of Bombay, recollected his childhood in the 1930s and 1940s: ‘In those days, the shift was 10 hours – from 5 pm to 3 am – terrible working hours.

The text explains that they established tea and coffee plantations, acquiring land at cheap rates from the colonial government; and they invested in mining, indigo and jute.

The text explains that how did the East India Company procure regular supplies of cotton and silk textiles from Indian weavers?

The text explains that imagine that you have been asked to write an article for an encyclopaedia on Britain and the history of cotton.

Source: NCERT Class 10 India and the Contemporary World II, Chapter 4: The Age of Industrialisation

prelims_traps: Do not treat 1 Before the Industrial Revolution and 1.1 The Coming Up of the Factory as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to The Age of Industrialisation; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain The Age of Industrialisation through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use 1 Before the Industrial Revolution and 1.1 The Coming Up of the Factory to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
